title: The End of the World
description: The End of the World — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "The End of the World" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: "Cold"
memes.description: "Could the world freeze to death as the sun grows cold?"

memes.label: "Comet"
memes.description: "Could a comet strike the earth and end all life?"

memes.label: "Crash"
memes.description: "Could the world be smashed by collision with another body?"

memes.label: "Drought"
memes.description: "Could the world die of thirst — oceans drained, rivers dry?"

memes.label: "Earth"
memes.description: "Could the earth perish while mankind somehow survives?"

memes.label: "Fire"
memes.description: "Could the world perish by fire — from within or without?"

memes.label: "God"
memes.description: "Could divine intervention bring about the end of the world?"

memes.label: "How?"
memes.description: "On the unknown origin of the world and the certainty of its end"

memes.label: "Man"
memes.description: "Could mankind perish before the earth itself?"

memes.label: "Never"
memes.description: "Could the world, against all odds, never end?"

memes.label: "Next Year"
memes.description: "If not this year, perhaps the next?"

memes.label: "Some Time"
memes.description: "The end may come at some distant, unknowable time"

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: "This Year"
memes.description: "Could the end come this very year?"

memes.label: "Water"
memes.description: "Could a great flood drown the world once more?"

memes.label: "What After?"
memes.description: "What, if anything, comes after the end of the world?"

memes.label: "When?"
memes.description: "If the world must end, when might it happen?"

memes.label: "Which First?"
memes.description: "If the world must end, which goes first — man or earth?"

memes.label: Adam
memes.description: The first man in biblical tradition, buried on Calvary according to Christian legend. The Memex corpus invokes Adam in connection with burial practices, the primordial sun, and the limits of antediluvian human stature.

memes.label: Adaptation
memes.description: "The capacity of living things — especially humans — to adjust to changing conditions, which Dennis presents as the great counter-force to cosmic doom: the reason the world's end may be delayed even if it cannot be prevented."

memes.label: Afterlife
memes.description: The belief that conscious existence continues after biological death — the universal religious answer to mortality that shapes every eschatological vision.

memes.label: Agrippina the Younger
memes.description: Mother of the Roman Emperor Nero, murdered at her son's command. The Memex corpus invokes her as witness to Nero's matricide — a crime catalogued among the horrors presaging apocalyptic judgment.

memes.label: "Albert Einstein"
memes.description: "The physicist whose theories of relativity established the finite, bounded cosmos that Dennis deploys as the decisive argument against an infinite, eternal universe."

memes.label: Alexander
memes.description: The Macedonian conqueror whose career, death, and the comet associated with his campaigns appear in Dennis's survey as examples of the ancient tendency to read celestial signs in political events.

memes.label: Alexander the Great
memes.description: King of Macedon and conqueror of the known world, invoked by the Memex corpus as a figure who "wanted to hold" the view that there were more worlds to conquer.

memes.label: America
memes.description: America as both a discovered "mistake" for Paradise and a central stage for end-of-the-world religious movements in Dennis's eschatological survey.

memes.label: Ammonite
memes.description: Ammonites — the spiral-shelled cephalopods that dominated Mesozoic seas — vanished at the same K-Pg boundary that claimed the dinosaurs, their exquisite fossil shells now serving as silent testimony to the universality of extinction.

memes.label: "Amos"
memes.description: "The eighth-century BCE Hebrew prophet whose declaration — 'the day of the Lord is darkness and not light' — Dennis cites as the archetypal prophecy of a dark and cold eschaton."

memes.label: "Andes"
memes.description: "Mountain range hosting Cuzco,symbol of solar worship at altitude. A geographical location referenced in Dennis's survey of world-ending mechanisms and their spatial implications."

memes.label: Annihilation
memes.description: The complete and utter destruction of existence — not death alone but the elimination of all trace, the reduction of being to nothing.

memes.label: "Antichrist"
memes.description: "The eschatological adversary figure who, in Christian apocalyptic tradition, opposes Christ and deceives the world before the final judgment — treated by Dennis through the lens of numerical prophecy and the persistent human compulsion to identify contemporary figures as Antichrist."

memes.label: Apocalypse
memes.description: The genre of revelatory literature and the mode of thinking that envisions a divinely ordained catastrophic end to the present world order, as examined by Dennis in his survey of end-of-the-world beliefs.

memes.label: "Apollo"
memes.description: "The Greek god of the sun, prophecy, and knowledge — a figure Dennis invokes in his survey of solar mythology and as a symbol of the intersection of divine revelation and cosmic catastrophe."

memes.label: "Arctic"
memes.description: "Polar region,first to freeze in rotational death scenario. A geographical location referenced in Dennis's survey of world-ending mechanisms and their spatial implications."

memes.label: Arctic Circle
memes.description: The Arctic Circle, referenced in the book as the birthplace of primates and as evidence of Earth's shifting climate zones — Peary "found Eden" there.

memes.label: Aristotle
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher, student of Plato, whose works shaped Western thought from antiquity through the Middle Ages. The Memex corpus names him among the greatest of the Greeks.

memes.label: Armageddon
memes.description: The prophesied final battle between the forces of good and evil at the end of the world — as a literal biblical site, a metaphor for terminal conflict, and a recurring cultural obsession that Dennis examines and subverts.

memes.label: "Astrological Doom"
memes.description: "Historical|The prediction of world-ending based on planetary conjunctions — such as the feared conjunction of 1524 — which Dennis examines as a case study in failed prophecy."
tags: Historical|The prediction of world-ending based on planetary conjunctions — such as the feared conjunction of 1524 — which Dennis examines as a case study in failed prophecy

memes.label: Astronomical Doom
memes.description: The category of end-of-world scenarios involving celestial bodies — comets, meteorites, stellar collisions, solar death — which Dennis surveys in the book's opening chapters as the most literally cosmic of apocalyptic threats.

memes.label: Atlantis
memes.description: Atlantis as Dennis's central geological metaphor — the sunken continent that embodies catastrophic earth-change, invoked alongside Gondwanaland and the Great Drift as evidence of planetary-scale destruction.

memes.label: "Atmospheric Collapse"
memes.description: "Geological|The thinning of earth's atmosphere to the point where it can no longer sustain life or retain heat — the quietest and most gradual of Dennis's end scenarios."
tags: Geological|The thinning of earth's atmosphere to the point where it can no longer sustain life or retain heat — the quietest and most gradual of Dennis's end scenarios

memes.label: "Atmospheric Loss"
memes.description: "The slow dissipation of the earth's atmosphere into space — following the precedent of the moon — which Dennis identifies as a critical accelerator of planetary cooling and biological extinction."

memes.label: Augustus
memes.description: First Roman Emperor (63 BCE – 14 CE), cited in the book as an exemplar of cynical statecraft in the face of cosmic portents.

memes.label: "Babylon"
memes.description: "The Mesopotamian metropolis that was the ancient world's greatest city — and in Dennis's treatment, the birthplace of eschatological imagination: 'Jewish Apocalyptic, a stream from the waters of Babylon, flows from the Captivity,' the city whose fall became the template for every prophecy of imperial judgment and whose symbolic afterlife outlasted its physical ruin."

memes.label: Balder
memes.description: The Norse god of light and beauty whose death — foretold in prophecy and accomplished through the machinations of Loki — serves in Dennis's argument as a pagan parallel to Christian apocalyptic mythology and a type of the dying god.

memes.label: "Beast"
memes.description: "In apocalyptic literature, a symbolic figure of chaos, opposition to the divine, and end-time tyranny — treated by Dennis within his survey of numerical prophecy, where the Beast's number (666) becomes a key to calculating the date of the world's end."

memes.label: "Biblical Flood"
memes.description: "The universal deluge described in Genesis — Dennis's archetype of world-ending through water, the catastrophe that 'has never lacked supporters' (004-013) across three millennia of human imagination, and the mode of extinction that stands in permanent dialectic with fire, cold, and comet as one of the elemental paths to cosmic death."

memes.label: Black Death
memes.description: The Black Death of the fourteenth century, referenced in the book as an example of a catastrophe narrowly heralded by a celestial sign — a comet.

memes.label: Boadicea
memes.description: Queen of the British Iceni tribe who led a rebellion against Roman occupation. The Memex corpus catalogues her revolt among the wars and slaughters that marked the Roman imperial era.

memes.label: Brontosaurus
memes.description: Brontosaurus — the "thunder lizard," one of the largest land animals ever to exist — represents the triumph of sheer scale that, in Dennis's framework, provides no guarantee against extinction.

memes.label: Calvary
memes.description: The site of Christ's crucifixion outside Jerusalem — the geographical and theological center of Christian soteriology, which Dennis invokes in connection with the cosmic signs that reportedly accompanied Christ's death.

memes.label: "Catastrophe"
memes.description: "The cosmic-scale destructive event — stellar collision, planetary concussion — that Dennis examines as the ultimate end of the world, where the 'crowded streets of space' and the inevitability of astronomical catastrophe render human eschatologies provincial by comparison."

memes.label: "Celestial Mechanics"
memes.description: "The orderly patterns of planetary and stellar motion — orbits, rotations, and tides — which Dennis argues are both our protection against and eventual vehicle of the world's end."

memes.label: Chaos
memes.description: The primordial state of disorder and confusion preceding cosmic order, and the eschatological return to chaos envisioned as the end of all structure.

memes.label: Christian
memes.description: The religious tradition centered on Jesus Christ, whose eschatological expectations — from the early Church's imminent Apocalypse to medieval numerical prophecy — form the primary historical subject of Dennis's survey of end-of-world belief.

memes.label: Christianity
memes.description: The religious tradition whose eschatological expectations — the Parousia, the Millennium, the Dies Irae — Dennis treats as the most consequential engine of end-of-the-world belief in Western history.

memes.label: Climate Change
memes.description: The alteration of Earth's climate systems — whether toward warming or cooling — which Dennis anticipates in his survey of geological climate history as a mechanism by which the conditions of human life may be gradually or catastrophically withdrawn.

memes.label: Cold
memes.description: Cold as an eschatological mechanism — the death of Earth and life through gradual or sudden cooling, from stellar heat loss to ice ages, treated in Dennis's The End of the World as one of the principal modes of cosmic extinction.

memes.label: Cold Death
memes.description: The gradual extinction of all life and energy through the irreversible cooling of the universe — Dennis's preferred mode of world-end and the thermodynamic destiny toward which all other catastrophes ultimately converge.

memes.label: Collision
memes.description: The cosmic impact — of comets, asteroids, or entire stars — as the most direct and physically inevitable mechanism by which the world may end.

memes.label: Comet
memes.description: A celestial body whose appearance has been interpreted throughout history as an omen of disaster — and whose physical collision with Earth represents one of the most enduring scenarios for the end of the world.

memes.label: "Comet Impact"
memes.description: "Astronomical|The most ancient and culturally embedded collision scenario — a comet striking the earth — which Dennis traces from Chinese astrologers through medieval European prophecy."
tags: Astronomical|The most ancient and culturally embedded collision scenario — a comet striking the earth — which Dennis traces from Chinese astrologers through medieval European prophecy

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Complete index of all concepts in the End of the World wiki, sorted alphabetically

memes.label: Conflagration
memes.description: The destruction of the world by fire — a mode of eschatological ending in which the atmosphere or the entire planet combusts in a single catastrophic event.

memes.label: "Continental Subsidence"
memes.description: "Geological|The sinking of landmasses beneath the sea — a geological process that Dennis presents as a potential flood mechanism and a reminder that the earth's crust is not permanently stable."
tags: Geological|The sinking of landmasses beneath the sea — a geological process that Dennis presents as a potential flood mechanism and a reminder that the earth's crust is not permanently stable

memes.label: Corinth
memes.description: The ancient Greek city of Corinth, referenced in the book as a site of earthquake associated with a comet, and as the home city of Timoleon who took a comet as an auspicious sign for his expedition.

memes.label: "Cosmic Collision"
memes.description: "The hypothesis that the world could end through collision with another celestial body — comet, meteor, planet, or star."

memes.label: "Cosmos"
memes.description: "The totality of all that exists — matter, space, time, stars, and worlds — which Dennis examines as the stage upon which all end-of-world scenarios must play out, and whose own finitude or infinitude determines whether any final end is possible."

memes.label: Creation
memes.description: The act or process by which the world came into being — in the corpus, the theological framework that establishes divine authorship of the cosmos and its eventual unmaking.

memes.label: "Cuzco"
memes.description: "The capital of the Inca Empire, whose Temple of the Sun — covered in gold — Dennis cites as the pre-Columbian counterpart to Rome's solar cult, evidence that sun worship was a near-universal human response to the source of all terrestrial life."

memes.label: "Cyclical Cosmos"
memes.description: "The ancient and persistent idea that the universe goes through endless cycles of creation and destruction — which Dennis examines as a possible escape from entropy, and ultimately rejects."

memes.label: Damnation
memes.description: The state of eternal punishment or separation from God following the Last Judgment — the negative outcome of the eschatological drama and the dark counterpart to salvation.

memes.label: "Daniel"
memes.description: "The biblical prophet whose visions of world empires rising and falling, and whose calculations of the 'seventy weeks,' provided one of the most influential frameworks for chronological eschatology in the Judeo-Christian tradition."

memes.label: Death
memes.description: The cessation of life — individual, species-wide, and planetary — as the central concern around which Dennis organizes his survey of end-of-world scenarios.

memes.label: Decay
memes.description: The gradual process of deterioration and decline — the slow counterpart to sudden destruction, affecting individuals, civilizations, and the physical universe itself.

memes.label: "Decreation"
memes.description: "Mythological|The symmetrical reversal of Genesis — 'Fiat,a shake of the sceptre,and then Nothing' — which Dennis presents as the most radical supernatural vision of the end,in which the world is not destroyed but unmade."
tags: and then Nothing' — which Dennis presents as the most radical supernatural vision of the end
in which the world is not destroyed but unmade

memes.label: Design
memes.description: The inference from cosmic order to a cosmic designer — the argument that the universe's evident structure implies purpose — which Dennis treats as one of the most persistent but most philosophically contested responses to the prospect of cosmic meaninglessness.

memes.label: Determinism
memes.description: The philosophical doctrine that every event is necessitated by antecedent events and natural laws — making the end of the world not a possibility but an inevitability already latent in the present state of the universe.

memes.label: Dies Irae
memes.description: The Dies Irae — the Day of Wrath in Christian eschatology — appears in the book as the expected day of final judgment, whose anticipated signs were seen fulfilled in every generation.

memes.label: Dinosaur
memes.description: The dinosaurs — the dominant terrestrial vertebrates of the Mesozoic Era — appear in Dennis's The End of the World as the archetypal case of extinction by natural causes, evidence that planetary-scale death can occur without human agency or divine intervention.

memes.label: Diplodocus
memes.description: Diplodocus — the slender giant of the Jurassic, one of the longest dinosaurs ever discovered — represents the extreme specialization that, however successful in its own time, provides no protection when ecosystems collapse.

memes.label: "Disaster"
memes.description: "A sudden, large-scale destructive event — natural, celestial, or divinely ordained — that Dennis uses both as a literal end-of-world scenario and as a metaphor for the fragility of human civilization."

memes.label: "Divine Judgment"
memes.description: "Mythological|The end of the world as punishment for human wickedness — the most common eschatological motif across cultures,which Dennis surveys from Hebrew prophecy to modern moralism."
tags: Mythological|The end of the world as punishment for human wickedness — the most common eschatological motif across cultures
which Dennis surveys from Hebrew prophecy to modern moralism

memes.label: Dodo
memes.description: The dodo — the iconic symbol of human-caused extinction — appears in Dennis's catalogue of vanished creatures as the most recent and most pointed example: a species extinguished not by cosmic catastrophe but by human carelessness, embodying the quiet, unspectacular mode of extinction that may ultimately be our own.

memes.label: "Doomsday Prophecy"
memes.description: "The human impulse to predict the date and manner of the world's end — which Dennis surveys across cultures and centuries, treating it as a psychological constant rather than a cosmological question."

memes.label: Drought
memes.description: The prolonged absence of water — examined in the book as both a local catastrophe that has shaped civilizations and a potential mechanism of global extinction through ecological collapse.

memes.label: Earthquake
memes.description: Earthquakes as both a natural geological phenomenon and a recurring sign of impending world-end — from Aristotle's Achaean earthquake to biblical prophecy, culminating in Dennis's vision of full crustal collapse.

memes.label: Eden
memes.description: The biblical garden of primordial perfection — both a lost past and a hoped-for future — which Dennis invokes as the symbolic origin and destination of the eschatological imagination.

memes.label: Egypt
memes.description: Egypt as the site of the Nile Flood, home of an ancient priestly class, and a key reference point in Dennis's survey of global deluge traditions and early astronomical knowledge.

memes.label: End of the World
memes.description: The central organizing concept of Dennis's inquiry — the termination of the human world, whether by cosmic catastrophe, divine judgment, or gradual decay — examined across scientific, theological, and historical perspectives.

memes.label: "Entropy"
memes.description: "The universal tendency toward equalization of temperature and exhaustion of energy, which Dennis identifies as the ultimate thermodynamic fate of the universe."

memes.label: Environmental Collapse
memes.description: The breakdown of Earth's life-support systems through climate change, resource depletion, or ecological degradation — a mode of world-end that Dennis fleetingly registers as one possible future among many.

memes.label: Eschatology
memes.description: The theological study of last things — death, judgment, heaven, hell, and the final destiny of the cosmos — forming the doctrinal framework through which end-of-the-world narratives are interpreted.

memes.label: Eternity
memes.description: The concept of infinite or unending time, contrasted with geological and human timescales in Dennis's eschatological framework.

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: Complete index of all events in the End of the World wiki, sorted alphabetically

memes.label: Extinction
memes.description: The permanent cessation of a species or of all life — the biological endpoint that Dennis treats as the ultimate form of death and the frame within which every end-of-world scenario must ultimately be evaluated.

memes.label: Faith
memes.description: Belief without — or despite — empirical evidence, examined in the book as both a psychological necessity for confronting cosmic doom and a cognitive error that distorts clear perception of reality.

memes.label: Famine
memes.description: Famine as an enduring mode of mass death and a recurring sign of the end-times — from ancient drought-driven starvation to Dennis's vision of organized hunger as a man-made apocalypse.

memes.label: Fate
memes.description: The notion that events — including the world's end — are predetermined, whether by divine decree, causal necessity, or cosmic order.

memes.label: Fimbul-Winter
memes.description: In Norse mythology, the Great Winter that precedes Ragnarok — three successive winters with no summer between, invoked in the book as a mythological parallel to the scientific cold-death hypothesis.

memes.label: "Finite Universe"
memes.description: "The modern astronomical finding — which Dennis treats as decisive — that space is finite and bounded, a closed curve upon itself, with profound implications for the inevitability of the world's end."

memes.label: Fire
memes.description: Fire as an eschatological mechanism — the destruction of Earth and life through cosmic or planetary conflagration, treated in Dennis's The End of the World as one of the four elemental extinction pathways and the oldest of humanity's end-of-world fears.

memes.label: "Flood Myth"
memes.description: "The flood — or inundation — as one of the most ancient and culturally universal visions of world-ending, which Dennis examines both as a potential physical mechanism and as a psychological archetype."

memes.label: Free Will
memes.description: The capacity of rational agents to choose between alternative courses of action — the concept that makes moral judgment coherent and that stands in perpetual tension with both theological predestination and physical determinism.

memes.label: "Galactic Collision"
memes.description: "Astronomical|The merger of the Milky Way with another galaxy — a process astronomers now know will occur with Andromeda — which Dennis glimpses in his discussion of extragalactic nebulae."
tags: Astronomical|The merger of the Milky Way with another galaxy — a process astronomers now know will occur with Andromeda — which Dennis glimpses in his discussion of extragalactic nebulae

memes.label: Ganges
memes.description: A major river of the Indian subcontinent, referenced in the book as a key example of delta formation and as one of the regions threatened by catastrophic glacial melt.

memes.label: "Gautama Buddha"
memes.description: "The founder of Buddhism, whose vision of cosmic cycles lasting vast kalpas — and his moral interpretation of world-ending as karmic retribution — Dennis invokes as an Eastern counterpoint to Western eschatology."

memes.label: Gehenna
memes.description: Gehenna, the valley of fire in Jewish eschatology, appears in the book as a metaphor for the ultimate fire-destruction of the Earth — the comet's impact boring "huge holes through to Gehenna beneath."

memes.label: "Geological Time"
memes.description: "The vast timescale of earth and cosmic history — billions of years — against which human existence and the end of the world must be measured."

memes.label: "Germ-Plasm Death"
memes.description: "Biological|The subtle sterilization of life at the genetic level — which Dennis speculates could occur if the sun entered 'some field of unknown matter' that transformed its radiation."
tags: Biological|The subtle sterilization of life at the genetic level — which Dennis speculates could occur if the sun entered 'some field of unknown matter' that transformed its radiation

memes.label: Germany
memes.description: Germany appears in the book as one of the European regions that would be inundated by catastrophic meltwater from the Alps, and as a place where apocalyptic millennial fears held less sway than in America.

memes.label: "Giordano Bruno"
memes.description: "The Renaissance philosopher and cosmologist, burned for heresy in 1600, whose vision of an infinite universe populated by innumerable worlds Dennis invokes as a daring precursor to modern cosmology."

memes.label: Great Flood
memes.description: The Great Flood — a universal deluge myth found across world traditions — appears in the book as the last in the Maya sequence of four world-destroying Ages, and as an archetype of the Water-End.

memes.label: Great Persecution
memes.description: The Great Persecution of early Christians by the Roman Empire, referenced in the book as one of the "signs" of the End whose fulfillment did not bring about the expected Second Coming.

memes.label: Greek
memes.description: The civilization and intellectual tradition of ancient Greece, which Dennis invokes as the source of both scientific cosmology and mythological eschatology — the "romantic" counterpoint to Roman practicality.

memes.label: Greenland
memes.description: Greenland, once green and fertile according to the fossil record, invoked in the book as evidence that Earth's climate has undergone radical and irreversible change.

memes.label: Harmony
memes.description: The ancient and medieval concept of a cosmic order — the "music of the spheres" — which Dennis invokes as a counterpoint to the chaos of apocalyptic destruction and as evidence for the providential design of the universe.

memes.label: "Heat Death"
memes.description: "Thermodynamic|The Wärmetod — the ultimate thermodynamic equilibrium in which all energy is uniformly distributed and no work can be done — the final state of Dennis's finite universe."
tags: Thermodynamic|The Wärmetod — the ultimate thermodynamic equilibrium in which all energy is uniformly distributed and no work can be done — the final state of Dennis's finite universe

memes.label: Heaven
memes.description: The celestial realm — both physical sky and theological dwelling-place of the divine — as it figures in Dennis's treatment of cosmic signs and final destruction.

memes.label: Hope
memes.description: The expectation of a positive outcome — in Dennis's treatment, a deeply ambivalent human disposition that ranges from the concrete hope of surviving catastrophe to the theological hope of millennial redemption, both of which the book subjects to skeptical scrutiny.

memes.label: "Human Progress"
memes.description: "The argument — which Dennis interrogates and ultimately rejects — that human civilization can progress indefinitely and thereby escape the cosmic doom awaiting the rest of nature."

memes.label: Ice
memes.description: The frozen form of water that has repeatedly covered continents — and whose return, in the form of a new ice age, represents one of the most scientifically grounded scenarios for global extinction.

memes.label: "Ice Age"
memes.description: "The periodic advance of continental ice sheets that Dennis invokes as both geological fact and eschatological omen — the 'vast flood of frozen water' (004-014) that has repeatedly extinguished species and, in his reading, demonstrates that the Earth's climate can undergo radical, life-obliterating transformations with no warning and no appeal."

memes.label: Ichthyosaur
memes.description: Ichthyosaurs — the dolphin-shaped marine reptiles that ruled Mesozoic seas before vanishing mysteriously in the mid-Cretaceous — embody Dennis's theme of extinction without obvious cause, the quiet disappearance of the once-dominant.

memes.label: "Immanuel Kant"
memes.description: "The German Enlightenment philosopher whose arguments about the limits of reason, the nature of time and space, and the antinomies of cosmology Dennis invokes in his philosophical framing of eschatological questions."

memes.label: India
memes.description: India as a geographical marker for Paradise's imagined location eastward, and as the source of ancient religious ideas about world-cycles in Dennis's survey.

memes.label: "Infinity"
memes.description: "The concept of boundlessness — spatial, temporal, and mathematical — that Dennis confronts in his final chapter as the ultimate epistemological limit: both finite and infinite universes are 'totally equally irrevocably inconceivable,' and the question of infinity is the question that reason cannot answer but cannot stop asking."

memes.label: Isaiah
memes.description: Hebrew prophet whose visions of fiery judgment and apocalyptic transformation are cited in the corpus eschatological imagery

memes.label: "Jerusalem"
memes.description: "The holy city of three faiths and the geographical center of biblical eschatology — the place where, in Dennis's treatment, heavenly signs pointed like a 'titan sword' (002-012), where prophets awaited the end, and whose destruction by Titus became the template for every subsequent expectation of world-ending judgment."

memes.label: "Jesus Christ"
memes.description: "The central figure of Christianity, whose prophecies of the end and return feature prominently in Dennis's survey of apocalyptic expectations."

memes.label: John the Apostle
memes.description: Apostle and author of the Book of Revelation, described by the corpus as "the Hellenized philosopher" among the Evangelists. The Memex corpus calls upon John's "Behold, I come quickly" as a pillar of apostolic eschatology.

memes.label: Joseph Wolff
memes.description: 19th-century Jewish-Christian missionary and millenarian who awaited the end in Jerusalem

memes.label: Joshua V. Hines
memes.description: Elder in the Millerite movement of the 1840s, a leading organizer who managed the "business side" of the Adventist expectation of Christ's return. The Memex corpus depicts him as a figure who derived both delight and material benefit from the movement's excitements.

memes.label: Judgment
memes.description: The divine or cosmic assessment of human conduct and worth, culminating in the eschatological separation of the righteous from the wicked — treated by Dennis as the moral frame into which all other end-of-world scenarios are fitted.

memes.label: Julio-Claudian
memes.description: The first imperial dynasty of Rome — Augustus, Tiberius, Caligula, Claudius, Nero — whose reigns Dennis invokes in connection with cometary portents, imperial decadence, and the political context of early Christian apocalypticism.

memes.label: Julius Caesar
memes.description: Roman statesman, military general, and dictator whose assassination and the comet of 43 BCE were interpreted as cosmic signs within the eschatological imagination of late antiquity.

memes.label: "Jupiter"
memes.description: "The chief god of the Roman pantheon, associated with thunder, sky, and cosmic order — whom Dennis invokes as a symbol of divine power over the elements, particularly fire."

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: The principle of moral order — divine or cosmic — whose perceived absence in the present world drives the demand for a final judgment in which wrongs are righted, a theme Dennis treats as one of the deepest psychological roots of eschatological belief.

memes.label: London
memes.description: London as a site of catastrophic events — plague, fire, and comet panic — that Dennis uses to illustrate how urban disasters fuel apocalyptic expectation across centuries.

memes.label: Love
memes.description: The profound affection and concern for another — in Dennis's treatment, one of the few human experiences that seems to resist reduction to cosmic indifference, yet ultimately as transient as everything else in an entropic universe.

memes.label: "Mars"
memes.description: "The fourth planet from the sun — a cold, arid world with a thin atmosphere — which Dennis references as an astronomical neighbor and possible preview of earth's own eventual fate."

memes.label: Martin Luther
memes.description: German theologian and Protestant Reformer (1483–1546), cited in the book as a figure who "hurried to finish his Bible" under apocalyptic time-pressure.

memes.label: Mary of Bethezub
memes.description: Woman mentioned in the book's account of the siege of Jerusalem, a symbol of ultimate desperation

memes.label: Mastodon
memes.description: The mastodon — an extinct proboscidean of the Pleistocene — appears in Dennis's The End of the World as evidence that even the mightiest beasts are vulnerable to extinction, a data point in his cumulative argument for the universality of species death.

memes.label: Megalodon
memes.description: Megalodon — the largest predatory shark in Earth's history, a marine apex predator of unprecedented scale — demonstrates that even the ocean's supreme hunter cannot survive when its world changes.

memes.label: Megatherium
memes.description: Megatherium — the giant ground sloth, one of the largest land mammals ever to exist — represents the vulnerability of even the most imposing megafauna to the environmental shifts that accompanied the end of the last Ice Age.

memes.label: Mercy
memes.description: The attribute of compassion and forbearance, often invoked in eschatological contexts as the final hope against divine judgment and cosmic destruction.

memes.label: Messiah
memes.description: The expected deliverer whose arrival inaugurates the end of history — the figure around whom Jewish and Christian apocalyptic expectations converge and divide.

memes.label: Meteor Shower
memes.description: Meteor showers as a recurring celestial spectacle and, in Dennis's cosmology, a possible agent of world-ending — from the meteoric theory of planetary formation to the lethal bombardments that could reduce Earth to dust.

memes.label: "Meteor Threat"
memes.description: "The possibility — which Dennis weighs and ultimately dismisses for the world as a whole — that meteors and bolides could end life on earth through impact alone."

memes.label: "Milky Way"
memes.description: "Our home galaxy — a vast disk of some hundred billion stars — which Dennis deploys as a unit of measurement in his attempt to convey the scale of the finite but incomprehensibly large universe."

memes.label: Millennium
memes.description: The prophesied thousand-year reign of Christ on earth (Revelation 20), and the chronological framework that drove a millennium of Christian date-setting — examined by Dennis as the most influential number in the history of apocalyptic expectation.

memes.label: Millerite
memes.description: The religious movement that formed around William Miller's prediction of Christ's return in 1844 — Dennis's central case study of mass prophetic enthusiasm, its social dynamics, and its survival beyond the Great Disappointment.

memes.label: Mortality
memes.description: The condition of being subject to death — the fundamental human fact that Dennis treats as the psychological root of every eschatology: the world must end because we must end, and we cannot bear to end alone.

memes.label: Moses
memes.description: Prophet and lawgiver who received the Ten Commandments, cited in the corpus as an archetype of divine judgment and moral authority

memes.label: "Mount Ararat"
memes.description: "The mountain in eastern Anatolia where, according to Genesis, Noah's Ark came to rest after the flood — the geographical anchor of the most famous survival narrative in eschatological literature."

memes.label: "Mount Wilson Observatory"
memes.description: "The astronomical observatory in California whose 100-inch telescope — the largest in the world when Dennis wrote — revealed the scale of the visible universe, providing the empirical foundation for his argument about cosmic finitude."

memes.label: "Nebular Hypothesis"
memes.description: "The scientific theory — first formulated by Laplace — that the solar system formed from a rotating cloud of incandescent gas, which Dennis treats as the archetype of scientific accounts of cosmic origin and, by symmetry, of cosmic end."

memes.label: Nero
memes.description: Roman Emperor (37–68 CE), archetype of the Antichrist in early Christian apocalyptic thought, cited in the book as the embodiment of apocalyptic evil.

memes.label: "Noah"
memes.description: "The biblical patriarch who survived the great deluge — Dennis's archetypal survivor of world-ending catastrophe, the one man spared when 'the waters prevailed exceedingly upon the earth,' and the psychological anchor that makes the thought of total annihilation bearable by imagining a remnant."

memes.label: Nothingness
memes.description: The state of absolute nonexistence — the void that precedes existence and to which, in certain eschatologies, the world ultimately returns — a concept that haunts Dennis's survey as the unspoken alternative to every vision of cosmic purpose.

memes.label: "Nova Explosion"
memes.description: "Astronomical|A star suddenly flaring to brilliance — which Dennis invokes in his collisional origin theory,where a dead star revived by impact 'flamed into new life,a glorious nova'."
tags: Astronomical|A star suddenly flaring to brilliance — which Dennis invokes in his collisional origin theory
where a dead star revived by impact 'flamed into new life

memes.label: Nuclear Winter
memes.description: A hypothesized global climatic catastrophe following large-scale nuclear war — smoke and soot from firestorms block sunlight, causing agricultural collapse and mass starvation, representing one of the fastest anthropogenic routes to world-end.

memes.label: "Order"
memes.description: "The structural principle — cosmic, moral, and mathematical — that Dennis examines as both a genuine feature of the universe ('some order in the star-streams') and a persistent human projection, where the evidence of design is 'considerable' precisely to minds that forget their own cage-bound perspective."

memes.label: "Organic Extinction"
memes.description: "The complete disappearance of all life from the earth — which Dennis distinguishes from the destruction of the physical planet itself as a separate, possibly earlier, and philosophically distinct event."

memes.label: "Paracelsus"
memes.description: "The Renaissance physician and alchemist whose speculative cosmology Dennis invokes as an example of pre-modern scientific imagination of world-ending through elemental forces."
aliases: Philippus Aureolus Theophrastus Bombastus von Hohenheim

memes.label: Paradise
memes.description: The concept of an original or ultimate perfect state — the lost garden of Eden and the hoped-for destination at the end of history, as it appears in Dennis's survey of end-of-the-world scenarios.

memes.label: "Paul the Apostle"
memes.description: "The most influential theologian of early Christianity, whose epistles shaped the Christian expectation of Christ's imminent return — an expectation Dennis examines as a case study in deferred eschatology."

memes.label: Perish
memes.description: The act of ceasing to exist — the terminal state that awaits individuals, species, civilizations, and potentially the entire world in the corpus's eschatological vision.

memes.label: "Persia"
memes.description: "The great empire of the ancient Near East, whose dualistic Zoroastrian eschatology — with its vision of a final battle between light and darkness — Dennis cites as one of the most influential sources of apocalyptic thought in the Western tradition."

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Complete index of all persons in the End of the World wiki, sorted alphabetically

memes.label: Simon Peter
memes.description: Chief of the Apostles, martyred at Rome. The Memex corpus invokes his declaration that "the end of all things is at hand" and his image of Christ's return coming "as a thief in the night."

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: Complete index of all places in the End of the World wiki, sorted alphabetically

memes.label: Plague
memes.description: Plague and pestilence as recurring modes of mass death and eschatological portents — from the Black Death of the 990s to the thirty thousand dead of the Urbs, culminating in Dennis's assessment of disease as one of the four classical end-time signs.

memes.label: "Plato"
memes.description: "The Athenian philosopher whose cosmological dialogue Timaeus — with its vision of a created cosmos subject to decay — Dennis invokes as the fountainhead of Western philosophical eschatology."

memes.label: Plesiosaur
memes.description: Plesiosaurs — the long-necked marine reptiles of the Mesozoic seas — demonstrate that extinction operates with equal finality in every domain, terrestrial and aquatic alike, leaving no realm of life exempt from its reach.

memes.label: Poppaea Sabina
memes.description: Second wife of the Roman Emperor Nero, kicked to death while pregnant. The Memex corpus records her fate among the catalogue of Nero's domestic crimes.

memes.label: Predestination
memes.description: The doctrine that all events — including the ultimate fate of every soul — have been eternally decreed by God, making the end of the world not a contingency but an inevitability woven into the fabric of creation from the beginning.

memes.label: "Prophecy"
memes.description: "The practice of predicting future events — particularly the date and manner of the world's end — through interpretation of sacred texts, numerical calculation, or claimed divine revelation, which Dennis surveys as a two-thousand-year history of recurrent failure."

memes.label: Providence
memes.description: The doctrine of divine guidance and care, invoked ironically in the book to question whether a perfect world would be destined for destruction.

memes.label: "Providential Design"
memes.description: "The belief that the world's end is determined by divine will — God, or the gods — rather than by natural causes alone."

memes.label: Pterodactyl
memes.description: Pterosaurs — the first vertebrates to achieve powered flight — dominated the skies for over 160 million years before vanishing at the K-Pg boundary, demonstrating that even the conquest of an entirely new dimension of existence provides no escape from extinction.

memes.label: Purification
memes.description: The concept of destruction as cleansing — the world's end not as annihilation but as the removal of corruption — which Dennis identifies as a recurring theme in fire-based eschatologies from Zoroastrianism to Christian judgment.

memes.label: "Ra"
memes.description: "The ancient Egyptian sun god — one of the oldest and most widespread solar deities in human history — whom Dennis invokes as the archetypal symbol of the sun's dual nature as life-giver and potential destroyer."

memes.label: Ragnarok
memes.description: The Norse mythological apocalypse — a sequence of catastrophic events culminating in a great battle, the death of the gods, the destruction of the world by fire and flood, and its eventual rebirth from the sea.

memes.label: Resurrection
memes.description: The doctrine that the dead will be raised to life at the end of time — the Christian answer to death's apparent finality and the mechanism by which individual persons survive the destruction of the world.

memes.label: "Revelation"
memes.description: "The disclosure of hidden knowledge — particularly of the end-times — whether through direct divine communication or through interpretive decoding of sacred texts, which Dennis examines both as a theological concept and as a recurring pattern in eschatological movements."

memes.label: "Nile"
memes.description: "Egypt's lifeline,referenced in drought/flood discussions. A geographical location referenced in Dennis's survey of world-ending mechanisms and their spatial implications."

memes.label: Roman
memes.description: The civilization of ancient Rome, which Dennis invokes repeatedly as a historical case study in imperial decline, cometary portents, numerical prophecy, and the cultural context within which early Christian eschatology took shape.

memes.label: "Rome"
memes.description: "The capital of the Roman Empire and one of the ancient world's great cities — which Dennis invokes as a symbol of imperial solar worship and the inevitable decline of even the mightiest civilizations."

memes.label: "Rotational Death"
memes.description: "Astronomical|The gradual slowing of earth's rotation through tidal braking — Dennis's most technically vivid end scenario,in which one hemisphere freezes in permanent darkness."
tags: Astronomical|The gradual slowing of earth's rotation through tidal braking — Dennis's most technically vivid end scenario
in which one hemisphere freezes in permanent darkness

memes.label: "Sahara"
memes.description: "The world's largest hot desert, once a fertile grassland — which Dennis invokes as geological evidence that climates change radically and that the conditions sustaining human civilization are not permanent."

memes.label: Salvation
memes.description: The theological concept of deliverance from sin and its consequences — the positive counterpoint to the book's catalogue of destruction, implied through references to Christ as Redeemer.

memes.label: Second Coming
memes.description: The prophesied return of Jesus Christ to earth — the central event of Christian eschatology that Dennis traces from the apostolic "at hand" through two millennia of deferred expectation, reinterpretation, and critical scholarship.

memes.label: Seth
memes.description: The third son of Adam and Eve in Genesis, to whom Adam reportedly described the lost splendors of Eden — a figure who appears in Dennis's survey as a link in the chain of antediluvian memory.

memes.label: Sibyl
memes.description: Prophetess of classical antiquity whose oracular books were consulted for signs of the end

memes.label: Sin
memes.description: The theological concept of moral transgression against divine law — presented in the corpus as both a cause of and context for apocalyptic destruction.

memes.label: Smilodon
memes.description: Smilodon — the saber-toothed cat, with its monstrous canines and powerful build — exemplifies how even the most specialized and effective predators cannot survive the collapse of their prey base and ecosystem.

memes.label: "Solar Death"
memes.description: "Astronomical|The terminal phase of the sun's evolution — whether through cooling or expansion — which Dennis treats as the single most certain mode of world-ending,the one catastrophe that even the most skeptical scientist cannot dismiss."
tags: Astronomical|The terminal phase of the sun's evolution — whether through cooling or expansion — which Dennis treats as the single most certain mode of world-ending
the one catastrophe that even the most skeptical scientist cannot dismiss

memes.label: "Solar Evolution"
memes.description: "The lifecycle of the sun — from formation through its eventual cooling or expansion — as the primary determinant of the earth's long-term habitability and ultimate fate."

memes.label: Solomon
memes.description: Biblical king of Israel, builder of the First Temple in Jerusalem, renowned for wisdom

memes.label: Soul
memes.description: The immaterial essence of a living being — in Dennis's treatment, that which faces annihilation or survival in the world's end.

memes.label: Space
memes.description: The boundless three-dimensional expanse in which cosmic objects exist and events occur — in Dennis's treatment, both the physical arena of celestial mechanics and a philosophical counterpoint to eternity.

memes.label: Species Index
memes.description: Complete index of extinct and prehistoric species documented in the wiki, ordered alphabetically with quality indicators.

memes.label: Star
memes.description: The fundamental unit of cosmic organization — stars, including our sun — whose life cycles, collisions, and eventual deaths Dennis surveys as the astronomical framework within which all terrestrial eschatology is ultimately contained.

memes.label: Stegosaurus
memes.description: Stegosaurus — with its distinctive plates and spiked tail — represents the extreme morphological specialization that makes a species exquisitely adapted to one world and utterly defenseless when that world ends.

memes.label: "Stellar Collision"
memes.description: "Astronomical|The impact of one star into another — the most violent of all cosmic catastrophes — which Dennis identifies as the only force capable of physically annihilating the earth rather than merely extinguishing its life."
tags: Astronomical|The impact of one star into another — the most violent of all cosmic catastrophes — which Dennis identifies as the only force capable of physically annihilating the earth rather than merely extinguishing its life

memes.label: "Stonehenge"
memes.description: "The Neolithic stone circle on Salisbury Plain — one of the oldest astronomically aligned monuments in the world — which Dennis includes in his catalogue of ancient solar temples as evidence of the deep antiquity of human preoccupation with the sun."

memes.label: Emanuel Swedenborg
memes.description: Swedish mystic and theologian (1688–1772), cited in the book as a representative of the theological tradition that envisions inhabited worlds beyond Earth.

memes.label: Tertullian
memes.description: Early Christian apologist and Church Father (c. 155 – c. 220 CE), cited in the book as a prominent chiliast — a believer in the literal millennium.

memes.label: The End of the World
memes.description: The book's title concept and organizing theme — the termination of the human world examined through every lens Dennis can bring to bear: scientific, theological, historical, and philosophical.

memes.label: Theodicy
memes.description: The philosophical problem of reconciling divine goodness with the existence of evil — a dilemma that Dennis treats as the unstated metaphysical engine driving the demand for a final judgment at the end of history.

memes.label: Time
memes.description: The dimension of sequential existence — in Dennis's usage, both the vast geological and cosmic timescale that renders human history a fleeting moment, and the eschatological horizon at which time itself may end.

memes.label: Top References
memes.description: Most referenced pages across all types in the End of the World wiki

memes.label: Tribulation
memes.description: The period of unprecedented suffering prophesied to precede the end of the world — examined by Dennis as both a theological concept and a recurring historical experience that each generation interprets as uniquely terminal.

memes.label: Triceratops
memes.description: Triceratops — the three-horned herbivore of the Late Cretaceous, one of the last non-avian dinosaurs to exist — was among the final witnesses to the K-Pg extinction, its fossil record ending precisely at the boundary where the Mesozoic world died.

memes.label: Trilobite
memes.description: Trilobites — among the most successful organisms in Earth's history, persisting for nearly 300 million years — demonstrate that even the longest-running success story in the fossil record ends in extinction.

memes.label: Tyrannosaurus Rex
memes.description: Tyrannosaurus rex — the most iconic of the dinosaurs and the largest terrestrial predator ever to walk the Earth — embodies the paradox that Dennis explores throughout: supreme power offers no protection against extinction.

memes.label: "Universal Death"
memes.description: "The terminal state of the entire universe — not merely the earth or the solar system — when all energy is exhausted, all motion ceases, and nothing remains but darkness, silence, and cold. Dennis treats this as the logical conclusion of thermodynamic reasoning and the ultimate horizon of all eschatological thought."

memes.label: Velociraptor
memes.description: Velociraptor — a small but intelligent predatory dinosaur — represents the paradox that even the most cognitively advanced species of its era could not out-think extinction when the K-Pg boundary arrived.

memes.label: "Venus"
memes.description: "The brightest planet in earth's sky — named for the goddess of love but, in reality, a hellish world of crushing pressure and furnace temperatures — which Dennis invokes as an example of how planetary conditions can diverge radically from what mythology imagines."

memes.label: "Virtue"
memes.description: "The moral quality of righteousness, often associated with purification through suffering or fire — treated by Dennis in his exploration of fiery eschatology, where virtue's purifying power becomes both a theological claim and an anthropological observation about human attitudes toward destruction."

memes.label: Volcano
memes.description: Volcanic eruptions as the visible symptom of Earth's internal fire — from Vesuvius burying Pompeii to Dennis's vision of a thousand reawakened volcanoes erupting in unison, consuming all life in three minutes.

memes.label: War
memes.description: War as humanity's self-inflicted mode of mass death — from Caesar's civil wars to the trenches of the First World War, culminating in Dennis's vision of war as proof that human beings need no cosmic accident to destroy themselves.

memes.label: Water
memes.description: The primordial element whose dual nature — life-giver and world-destroyer — makes it the most symbolically charged agent of apocalyptic transformation in the corpus.

memes.label: William Miller
memes.description: American farmer and Baptist preacher (1782-1849) whose prediction that Christ would return in 1844 launched the Millerite movement and culminated in the Great Disappointment — Dennis's central case study of modern prophetic failure.

memes.label: "William Shakespeare"
memes.description: "The English poet and playwright, whose lines Dennis occasionally quotes or alludes to as a source of wisdom about human mortality and cosmic impermanence."

memes.label: Woolly Mammoth
memes.description: The woolly mammoth — an extinct proboscidean of the Pleistocene — appears in Dennis's treatment of extinction as evidence preserved in ice, its frozen carcasses in Siberia serving as material proof that entire species can vanish from the Earth.

memes.label: Zion
memes.description: Mount Zion, the hill in Jerusalem sacred to Judaism and Christianity, referenced in the book as the site of the Roman destruction of Jerusalem — a historical apocalypse that prefigures the final one.
